title: Falls Prevention Technology: A Buyer's Guide for 2026
description: A 2026 buyer's guide to falls prevention technology. Compare ambient monitoring, wearables, and clinical screening tools.

blocks.question: What types of falls prevention technology are available and how do they differ?
blocks.directAnswer: Falls prevention technology falls into five categories: ambient monitoring (cameras and sensors), wearable alert systems, clinical screening tools (digital assessments), EHR-embedded checklists, and exercise or balance training platforms. Each addresses different aspects of falls risk. Most organizations use multiple categories in combination.

tldrBullets.text: Ambient AI systems detect falls in real-time using cameras or floor sensors for rapid response
Clinical screening tools identify residents at high risk before falls occur, enabling preventive interventions
EHR integration embeds basic screening checklists into existing workflows, though depth varies significantly

blocks.expandedAnswer:
The falls prevention technology landscape has expanded dramatically as evidence accumulated on fall reduction benefits. Vendors now offer solutions spanning detection, prediction, prevention, and recovery. Understanding these categories helps organizations choose systems aligned with their specific needs and risk populations.

Ambient monitoring systems (SafelyYou, VirtuSense) use video analytics or floor sensors to detect falls instantly and alert staff. These systems excel at rapid response but do not predict falls beforehand. Clinical screening tools (Able Assess, Linus Health, Zibrio) use validated assessments to identify high-risk residents proactively. EHR platforms (PointClickCare, MatrixCare) now embed basic screening checklists, though clinical depth varies. Exercise platforms provide targeted balance training. Wearables alert when residents are at risk of falling.

Effective falls prevention typically combines approaches. Organizations might use clinical screening to identify high-risk residents, targeted exercise programs for those with balance deficits, and ambient monitoring in high-acuity units. The key is selecting complementary technologies rather than overlapping systems.

sources.title: Ambient monitoring systems for fall detection in aged care
Clinical screening tools for falls risk prediction in older adults
Technology adoption and falls reduction outcomes in residential care

blocks.body:
Ambient monitoring: Real-time fall detection

Ambient AI systems use cameras, floor pressure sensors, or radar-based motion detection to identify falls as they occur and alert staff immediately. Leading systems include SafelyYou, which uses overhead camera AI, and VirtuSense, which uses wearable sensors. These systems excel at rapid detection, critical in high-acuity settings where delayed response increases injury severity.

Ambient systems work well in communal areas like dining rooms and activity spaces where surveillance is expected. They struggle in private rooms due to privacy regulations and resident consent requirements. Installation requires IT infrastructure (network cameras, server capacity) and facility infrastructure planning (ceiling-mounted camera angles, network connectivity).

Strengths of ambient systems: instant fall detection, rapid staff alert, reduced response time, documented evidence of fall circumstances. Limitations: high upfront capital cost, ongoing maintenance, privacy considerations, no predictive capability (detects falls after they occur, not before). Most effective in combination with predictive screening to prevent falls before they happen.

Wearable alert systems

Wearable fall risk systems (RxSight, SafetyLink) monitor residents during ambulation and alert staff when balance becomes unstable or resident posture suggests imminent fall risk. Some systems use accelerometers and gyroscopes; others use computer vision on tablets or phones that residents carry. Effectiveness depends on consistent device wear and staff responsiveness to alerts.

Wearables work best for residents with cognitive ability to understand device purpose and motivation to wear the device. Residents with advanced dementia often refuse wearables, limiting applicability. Battery life varies from 8 hours to several days depending on system, creating charging logistics. Some wearables integrate with EHR systems, while others generate separate alert logs.

Strengths: predictive alerts before falls occur, portable, relatively low cost, staff training straightforward. Limitations: dependent on consistent device wear, limited effectiveness for cognitively impaired residents, alerts require staff action (effectiveness depends on staffing levels), variable integration with existing systems.

Clinical screening tools for risk identification

Digital clinical screening platforms (Able Assess, Linus Health, Zibrio) administer validated assessment batteries to identify high-risk residents before falls occur. These tools typically take 5 to 15 minutes and screen for multiple fall risk factors including balance deficits, muscle weakness, medication interactions, and environmental hazards. Results generate personalized intervention recommendations.

Able Assess uses standardized tests recommended by CDC STEADI, administered on tablets by care staff with minimal training. Linus Health uses voice analysis and cognitive testing to identify neurological fall risk factors. Zibrio focuses on gait and balance assessment. Each tool addresses overlapping but distinct aspects of fall risk.

Strengths of clinical screening tools: evidence-based assessment, identifies specific risk factors enabling targeted interventions, standardized protocols improve consistency, relatively low cost compared to ambient systems, results inform care planning directly. Limitations: require staff time for administration, require validated staff training, identify risk (don't prevent falls directly), no real-time detection capability.

EHR-embedded screening checklists

Major EHR platforms (PointClickCare, MatrixCare) now embed basic falls risk checklists into resident assessment workflows. These checklists typically screen for risk factors like medication side effects, vision impairment, and previous falls history. They integrate directly into resident records and can trigger alerts or care plan recommendations.

EHR-embedded screening offers convenient accessibility since staff already work within the platform. Checklists require no additional device investment or system training. However, depth varies significantly. Many EHR checklists are brief (5 to 10 factors) and lack the comprehensive assessment batteries of dedicated clinical screening tools. Some EHR systems offer integration with external screening platforms for deeper assessment.

Strengths: convenient workflow integration, low implementation cost, immediate documentation, supports SAFE Act compliance. Limitations: limited clinical depth, varies significantly by EHR vendor, risk stratification less sophisticated than dedicated tools, less evidence supporting proprietary checklist approaches.

Exercise and balance training platforms

Dedicated platforms (LivongO, Otago Exercise Program digital versions) provide targeted balance and strength training based on evidence that regular exercise reduces falls risk by 20 to 30 percent in older adults. These systems typically include video instruction, progress tracking, and adaptable difficulty levels based on resident capability.

Effective exercise programs require consistent engagement from residents, ideally three or more sessions per week. Digital platforms support this through reminders, progress tracking, and engaging interfaces. Group exercise classes can also be supported through digital instructor guidance. Results improve when programs are integrated into daily schedules rather than optional activities.

Strengths: reduces falls risk through evidence-based mechanism (strengthening and balance), improves secondary outcomes (mobility, confidence, social engagement), relatively low technology cost, enhances quality of life beyond falls reduction. Limitations: requires resident engagement and capability, results take weeks to materialize, requires staff support for program management.

How to choose the right technology for your organization

Start by assessing your current falls prevention maturity. Do you have validated screening protocols in place, or do staff rely on informal assessment? Are interventions targeted to identified risks, or generic across all residents? Are your highest-risk residents concentrated in specific units or distributed? These baseline questions determine technology priorities.

Define your desired outcomes. Are you prioritizing speed of detection (pointing toward ambient systems), early identification of high-risk residents (pointing toward clinical screening), or prevention through exercise (pointing toward training platforms)? Most organizations benefit from multiple technologies addressing different aspects, but resources usually limit initial implementation to one primary system.

Evaluate technology fit with your existing systems. Will ambient cameras integrate with your security infrastructure and privacy policies? Does your EHR support external clinical screening tool integration? Can wearables connect to your alert system? Technology choices cascade through your infrastructure, so integration assessment matters.

Consider implementation burden and staff capacity. Clinical screening tools require staff time per resident but relatively straightforward training. Ambient systems require significant IT infrastructure investment and installation planning. Exercise programs require dedicated staff or volunteer coordination. Honest assessment of your organization's change capacity prevents over-commitment.

Pilot before full implementation. Bring one system into a single unit for 4 to 12 weeks to assess actual utility, identify workflow disruptions, and gather staff feedback. Small-scale pilots surface issues that theoretical planning misses. This also builds staff confidence in technology before organization-wide rollout.

Measure outcomes rigorously. Track not just falls reduction (the ultimate goal) but also implementation metrics like screening completion rates, intervention initiation rates, and staff training success. Falls reduction takes months to demonstrate, so intermediate metrics help confirm the system is working as intended.

stats.stat: Clinical screening with targeted interventions reduces falls incidence by 20 to 30 percent in older adults living in residential care
Ambient fall detection systems reduce time to staff response from 15-20 minutes to under 2 minutes, significantly reducing injury severity
Regular exercise programs reduce falls risk by 25-30 percent and are among the most cost-effective falls prevention interventions
Digital clinical screening tools improve assessment standardization with inter-rater reliability of 0.85 or higher, versus 0.65-0.78 for paper assessment

items.question: Should we invest in ambient fall detection or clinical screening tools first?
items.answer: Clinical screening typically delivers better return on investment because it identifies high-risk residents and enables preventive interventions before falls occur. Ambient systems excel at rapid response but don't prevent falls. Most organizations implement clinical screening first, then add ambient systems in high-acuity units where rapid detection adds distinct value. Combine both for comprehensive protection.

items.question: Can we use our existing EHR falls screening checklist instead of buying a dedicated tool?
items.answer: It depends on your EHR's clinical depth. If your checklist covers comprehensive balance, strength, and functional assessment aligned with CDC STEADI standards, it may be sufficient. Many EHR checklists are brief and lack clinical depth. Compare your current checklist against evidence-based standards. If gaps exist, a dedicated clinical screening tool like Able Assess provides deeper assessment and better intervention recommendations.

items.question: What's the typical cost comparison between these technology categories?
items.answer: Clinical screening tools typically cost $600-$2,500 per facility per year depending on resident volume. Ambient systems cost $40,000-$125,000+ for installation and ongoing licensing depending on facility size and system type. EHR-embedded screening is often included in existing platform costs. Exercise platforms cost $2,500-$12,500 annually. Wearables range from $600 to $6,000 depending on resident count. Build a hybrid approach based on your budget and risk profile.

items.question: How do we measure whether our falls prevention technology is actually working?
items.answer: Track falls incidence and injury rates as primary outcomes (measured quarterly), but also monitor implementation metrics: screening completion rates (monthly), intervention initiation rates (monthly), and staff training compliance (at implementation and annually). Improvement in intermediate metrics suggests the system is working correctly; falls reduction follows within 2-3 months. Monitor outcomes rigorously for the first 12 months to justify ongoing investment.

items.question: What happens if residents refuse to use wearables or participate in exercise programs?
items.answer: Clinical screening and ambient systems work without resident participation, making them better choices for facilities with significant cognitive impairment. Wearables and exercise programs require resident engagement and may not suit all populations. Assess your resident population's cognitive and functional baseline. Hybrid approaches, combining non-participatory screening with optional exercise for cognitively intact residents, often work best.

blocks.heading: Ready to implement comprehensive falls prevention?
blocks.bodyText: Able Assess provides the clinical screening foundation for effective falls prevention technology strategy. Identify high-risk residents, enable targeted interventions, and build a falls reduction program supported by evidence. See how organizations reduce falls incidence by 25-30 percent with systematic screening and care planning.
